findings.title: Cascade: subprocess timeout orphans claude/codex children consuming CPU and tokens after harness gives up
evidence: Trigger: a single eval (e.g. a long input file or model retry storm) takes longer than the 900s timeout in run_claude or run_codex.
Step 1: subprocess.run raises TimeoutExpired. Per CPython docs, TimeoutExpired does NOT terminate the child; it only releases the parent's wait. The claude/codex process keeps running.
Step 2: TimeoutExpired propagates up through run_model into the future. In run_iteration's as_completed loop (line 2185-2201), the exception is caught and the worker is marked failed.

Step 3: ThreadPoolExecutor schedules the next job on that worker thread. The previously-orphaned claude subprocess is still consuming CPU, RAM, and (for paid models) burning tokens against the user's account.

Step 4: With --workers 8 and a recurring failure mode (e.g., a model that stalls on a particular input), the harness can accumulate 8+ orphaned subprocesses per iteration, all running for an unbounded duration.
Step 5: A second invocation of the harness (or `--no-reset` flow) starts new subprocesses while old ones are still running, doubling resource pressure.
Failure outcome: silent token burn, machine slowdown, and unkillable processes that only die when the user notices and runs `pkill claude` / `pkill codex`.
findings.recommendation: Wrap subprocess.run in try/except TimeoutExpired that calls proc.kill() (or use subprocess.Popen + communicate(timeout=) with explicit kill in finally). At minimum, catch TimeoutExpired in run_claude/run_codex and re-raise after killing.

findings.title: Resource leak: codex tempfile is never cleaned up when subprocess.run raises (timeout, signal, or pre-exec failure)
evidence: run_codex creates a NamedTemporaryFile with delete=False at line 1388 to give codex an --output-last-message path.
The cleanup path (output_path.unlink) is inside the try/finally block at lines 1412-1420. That try/finally only wraps the read of output_path AFTER subprocess.run returns.
Trigger: subprocess.run at line 1405 raises TimeoutExpired (timeout=900) or KeyboardInterrupt or OSError (e.g. codex binary missing).
Step 1: The exception escapes run_codex BEFORE reaching the try/finally that owns the unlink. The tempfile, ~/var/folders/.../humanise-codex-output-*.md, leaks.
Step 2: With repeated failures (a flaky model, ctrl-c during runs, dev iteration loops), /tmp accumulates one orphan per failed call.
Step 3: On macOS the temp dir is per-user and is cleared periodically, but on Linux CI runners /tmp can fill before clean-up.
Failure outcome: silent disk-space leak; under pathological retry loops can fill the volume.
findings.recommendation: Move the `try: ... finally: output_path.unlink(missing_ok=True)` to wrap the entire subprocess.run call, not just the read.

findings.title: Cancellation safety: Ctrl-C leaves up to 8 orphaned subprocesses and a partial workspace
evidence: Trigger: user hits Ctrl-C while run_iteration is mid-flight with --workers 8.
Step 1: KeyboardInterrupt is delivered to the main thread. ThreadPoolExecutor's __exit__ blocks on shutdown(wait=True) by default. It will not cancel pending futures until they return.
Step 2: Each worker thread is sitting inside subprocess.run(..., timeout=900). The signal does NOT propagate to those Popen children automatically; Python only delivers signals to the main thread.
Step 3: The main thread is now blocked inside ThreadPoolExecutor.__exit__ waiting for 8 workers, each waiting for its claude/codex child. Effective hang of up to 15 minutes per worker.
Step 4: Even after shutdown completes, the workspace at dev/skill-workspace/iteration-N has partial outputs: some response.md files written, some not, no benchmark.json, no performance-report.md.
Step 5: A subsequent rerun without --no-reset will shutil.rmtree the partial workspace; a rerun WITH --no-reset will mix old and new outputs (see adv-7).
Failure outcome: 'unresponsive Ctrl-C' that wastes 15 minutes of machine time, plus hidden mixed state on resume.
findings.recommendation: Install a SIGINT handler that propagates SIGTERM to running subprocesses (track them in a list / WeakSet), and pass cancel_futures=True to executor.shutdown on KeyboardInterrupt. Alternatively, use Popen + communicate so signal handlers can reach children directly.

findings.title: Stale-data poisoning: --no-reset path silently re-grades old run outputs against new assertions
evidence: Trigger: developer runs `--no-reset` after editing evals.json (changed assertions, renamed evals, or dropped/added evals).
Step 1: prepare_workspace skips shutil.rmtree because reset=False. Stale eval directories from prior runs remain.
Step 2: If the new evals.json has fewer entries than the prior run, the orphan eval-N-* directories are still on disk.
Step 3: grade_completed_runs (line 2203) iterates the NEW evals list — not the disk — so orphan directories are not graded. OK so far.

Step 4: BUT for an existing eval-id whose assertions list has changed, run_dir/outputs/response.md still exists from the prior run. grade_run loads stale response.md and grades it against new assertions — producing pass/fail signals on output the new prompt never produced.

Step 5: Worse: build_performance_report reads benchmark.json which was generated by aggregate_benchmark.py against the new run — but only over jobs in `jobs` list. If a worker failed silently last time and we're re-running just that one, the unmodified runs from last iteration get treated as 'this iteration's results' in the report.

Failure outcome: corpus_baseline / regression tables present misleading numbers attributed to the current iteration. The PR's core claim (deterministic across iterations) is invalidated by ordinary developer ergonomics.
findings.recommendation: When --no-reset is set, either (a) refuse to run if evals.json mtime > workspace mtime, or (b) per-eval-dir invalidate cached response.md whose timing.json is older than evals.json.

findings.title: Path traversal via evals.json item.name: attacker-controlled or careless eval name escapes the workspace
evidence: eval_dir_name returns f'eval-{item["id"]}-{item["name"]}'. item['name'] is a string field copied from evals.json with no sanitisation.
Trigger: an evals.json entry has name set to '../../etc/passwd' or '../../../private-key' or contains a slash.

Step 1: prepare_workspace at line 2151 builds `directory = ITERATION / eval_dir_name(item)`. With name='../foo', directory becomes WORKSPACE/iteration-1/eval-0-../foo == WORKSPACE/iteration-1/foo (one level up via .. resolution by the filesystem).

Step 2: directory.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) creates the directory outside ITERATION. Subsequent writes (eval_metadata.json, prompt.md, response.md, grading.json) land outside the intended workspace.

Step 3: if reset=True, shutil.rmtree(ITERATION) at line 2148 happens BEFORE the malformed mkdir, so the original target is wiped — but the new directory is created elsewhere and now persists across resets. Each iteration leaks files into ROOT or higher.
Step 4: With name=`/tmp/x` (absolute), Path() / '/tmp/x' resolves to '/tmp/x', writes happen in /tmp regardless of the workspace.

Failure outcome: eval outputs and metadata land outside the workspace; files can be overwritten arbitrarily within the user's permission set. Concrete on macOS/Linux. Mitigated only by evals.json being a repo file, but no defence-in-depth.
findings.recommendation: Validate item['name'] against ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$ in load time, or sanitise via re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9_-]', '_', name) inside eval_dir_name.

findings.title: Argv length overflow: a single corpus file >~1MB makes claude/codex invocation fail with E2BIG
evidence: build_prompt reads each input file into memory and embeds its full text into the prompt string. The prompt is then passed as an argv element: `claude -p <prompt>` or `codex exec ... <prompt>`.
POSIX ARG_MAX on macOS is ~1MB; on Linux it's typically 128KB-2MB. The total size of argv + envp must fit under ARG_MAX.
Trigger: someone adds a long-form essay or merged corpus file >256KB to evals.json `files`. Two such files combined push argv past ARG_MAX.
Step 1: subprocess.run raises OSError with errno E2BIG ('Argument list too long') BEFORE the child runs.

Step 2: run_claude/run_codex don't catch OSError. The exception escapes run_model and is caught by the as_completed loop's `except Exception` (line 2191), producing an ERROR response.md and a synthetic timing.json showing 0 duration.

Step 3: grade_run runs against 'ERROR: ...' content. catalogue_hits, audit_shape checks, regrade — all evaluate against the error string. Many programmatic checks will spuriously pass or fail (e.g., 'no-em-dashes' will pass; 'audit-flags-present' will fail; 'rewrite-produced' will fail).
Step 4: aggregate_benchmark.py and build_performance_report happily aggregate the bogus pass/fail signal. Iteration-N comparison will show a regression caused entirely by argv overflow.
Failure outcome: silent regression introduced by adding a longer corpus file; the actual root cause (argv overflow) appears nowhere in the report.
findings.recommendation: Pass long inputs via stdin (subprocess.run(..., input=prompt, text=True)) or via a temp file referenced by path; cap embedded file size with an explicit warning.

findings.title: Race-free but redundant: every grading.json is written twice per iteration; if grader code path differs the second write silently masks the first
evidence: In run_iteration, the as_completed loop calls grade_run(item, run_dir) at line 2190 for each successfully-completed worker, AND at line 2200 for each failed worker.
Then at line 2203, grade_completed_runs(evals) iterates ALL evals and re-runs grade_run on every (item, with_skill, run-1) and (item, old_skill, run-1) directory whose response.md exists.
Step 1: First write: grading.json written from inside the as_completed loop on the worker outcome (per-item).
Step 2: Second write: grade_completed_runs overwrites it. If between the two writes any state changed (timing.json modtime, response.md edited by hand mid-run), grade_run reads the new state.

Step 3: Anyone debugging will see only the second result. Diagnostic gymnastics: if the as_completed grading raises (e.g., one run produced a corrupt timing.json), grade_completed_runs runs anyway and silently 'fixes' the result.
Failure outcome: not a crash, but the second pass masks intermittent grading failures that would otherwise be visible.
findings.recommendation: Drop the call at line 2203, OR remove the per-future grade_run calls at lines 2190/2200. Pick one place to do the work.

findings.title: Determinism claim violated: changing humanise/grade.py thresholds makes 'iteration N' corpus baseline incomparable to 'iteration N-1' though the PR claims they are
evidence: The PR documents (line 1986, in the report markdown) and the task summary state that group means are 'deterministic — independent of how the skill renders its audit'.
But group means come from grade_sample_file(filepath) which calls GRADE.grade_file(...). GRADE is humanise/grade.py loaded fresh at module import every iteration.
Trigger: developer tunes a regex threshold in humanise/grade.py between iteration 3 and iteration 4 (the normal iteration loop).
Step 1: iteration 4 corpus_baseline is computed with the new grade.py rules; iteration 3's baseline (read from previous_iteration_dir/performance-report.json) was computed with the old rules.

Step 2: regression_rows compares per_eval pass rates only — but that depends on the grader too via 'rewrite-passes-balanced-grader' and 'rewrite-passes-all-grader' assertions (lines 1551-1559). A grader change alone causes a regression flag.
Step 3: build_performance_report's narrative claims 'deterministic across both iterations' but the code makes no version check on grade.py.
Step 4: The README performance block (line 2101-2138) inherits the misleading numbers and is auto-published to the project README.
Failure outcome: false regression alarms or false 'no regression' signals after a grader edit; the documented determinism claim is unenforceable.
findings.recommendation: Record GRADE module's git rev (or content hash) into performance-report.json; in regression rows, refuse to compare across mismatched grader hashes and emit a clear note.

findings.title: Empty-corpus crash path: a corpus group present in corpus.json but with all paths missing produces n=0 means and degrades downstream tables
evidence: load_corpus_groups silently swallows FileNotFoundError per path (line 1782-1785). A group with all paths missing returns an empty list rather than failing.
Trigger: a corpus.json refactor renames samples but forgets to update one group (e.g., 'ai_rewrite' paths all moved).
Step 1: corpus_data['groups']['ai_rewrite'] = [] (empty).
Step 2: group_rows['ai_rewrite'] never gets populated because corpus_rows iterates only paths that resolved (loop 1849-1858 iterates paths, which is empty).
Step 3: group_summary therefore omits 'ai_rewrite' entirely. Pairwise gaps section omits the group silently.

Step 4: BUT if load_corpus_groups is called and a non-human group has SOME paths but the human group ends up empty (paths all missing), `_gap_pct(o['total'], human['total'])` divides by 0; the function handles 0 (returns 'n/a'), but the pairwise_gaps dict in structured (line 2079-2089) computes gaps for groups other than 'human'. Edge-case path: `if 'human' in group_summary` — passes. But human['total'] is 0.0 because the group has 0 entries (after _mean returns 0 for empty list). All gaps become 'n/a'. Report renders meaningless table.
Step 5: Worse — there is no warning logged. The user sees the report and assumes the corpus baseline ran fine.
Failure outcome: silently misleading performance report on a stale corpus.
findings.recommendation: Log a warning when a group resolves to fewer paths than corpus.json declared. Refuse to render gap rows when n=0.

findings.title: Auth assumption: stripping ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from env breaks any environment that authenticates via API key, not subscription
evidence: run_claude builds env without ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: `env = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if k not in {'CLAUDECODE', 'ANTHROPIC_API_KEY'}}`.
Trigger: a user (or CI) authenticates claude CLI via the API key env var rather than `claude login` subscription.
Step 1: subprocess invocation lacks the key. claude CLI returns non-zero exit with auth-error message.
Step 2: run_claude detects proc.returncode != 0 and writes a synthetic 'ERROR: claude run failed' response.
Step 3: grade_run grades the error string — many checks fail or pass nonsensically.
Step 4: The performance report shows ~0% pass rate across all evals, and the regression table flags every eval as regressed. The actual cause (env stripped by harness) is invisible.
Failure outcome: a CI run that 'works on my machine' silently produces a broken report on a machine using API-key auth. No clear error path.
findings.recommendation: Either keep ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (preferred — it's the documented auth path) or, if the intent is to force interactive subscription auth, fail fast with a precondition check rather than running 8 subprocesses that all fail.

findings.title: Workspace re-entrancy: shutil.rmtree(ITERATION) races with a viewer subprocess from a previous run
evidence: When --static-viewer is NOT set, the script launches a long-lived viewer via subprocess.Popen at line 2240 and exits without waiting. The viewer reads files from ITERATION (benchmark.json, response.md, etc.).
Trigger: developer leaves the viewer running, edits the skill, runs the iteration script again with default --reset.
Step 1: shutil.rmtree(ITERATION) at line 2148 deletes the directory the viewer's webserver is serving.
Step 2: The viewer is serving a Flask/HTTP request from the user's browser; reads of deleted files raise FileNotFoundError, returning 500s.

Step 3: New iteration writes new files into the same path. The old viewer process is still bound to the old working dir / file handles; behaviour is undefined (may serve mixed old/new content depending on how the viewer caches).
Step 4: At minimum the user sees confusing data; at worst the viewer process needs manual restart.
Failure outcome: stale-server confusion across iteration runs.
findings.recommendation: On --reset, warn (or error) if a viewer.log exists with a recent mtime; or write the viewer's PID and SIGTERM it before rmtree.

findings.title: Subprocess output buffer growth: claude --verbose JSON output is fully buffered in memory by capture_output=True; 8 workers produce unbounded memory pressure
evidence: subprocess.run(..., capture_output=True, text=True) buffers all stdout and stderr in memory until the child exits.
claude with --verbose --output-format json emits one JSON-array element per tool call/message and can grow to hundreds of MB on a long run that uses Bash and Read heavily.
Trigger: --workers 8 with all 8 jobs hitting verbose claude in parallel.
Step 1: Each Python worker thread holds a Popen with growing stdout/stderr buffers; total RSS grows ~8x per-worker peak.
Step 2: On a 16GB MacBook, 8 * 1GB peak = 16GB; system swaps, then OOM killer (or activity slowdown).

Step 3: When a worker is OOM-killed mid-run, its tempfile (codex case) is leaked (see adv-2) and its grading.json never gets written. The harness exception path catches it (line 2191) — but the OOM may take down sibling workers too.
Failure outcome: large-corpus runs sporadically OOM or thrash; no diagnostic surface. Lower confidence because typical runs are smaller, but the design has no bound.
findings.recommendation: Stream stdout to a file with subprocess.Popen and a writer thread, or set a max-output-bytes budget per call.

findings.title: JSON-as-state assumption: malformed evals.json (duplicate keys / non-dict entry) crashes the whole iteration mid-run
evidence: read_json uses json.loads which silently keeps the LAST value for duplicate keys and accepts arbitrary structures.
Trigger: hand-edit of evals.json introduces a duplicate eval id, a missing 'name', or 'files' as a string instead of list.

Step 1: prepare_workspace iterates evals — if name is missing, eval_dir_name raises KeyError. The harness has no try/except around prepare_workspace; the iteration aborts with an opaque traceback before any work runs.

Step 2: If 'files' is a string instead of list, build_prompt iterates characters of the string and tries resolve_eval_file on each character. Each char is a 1-char path; resolve_eval_file raises FileNotFoundError. The exception escapes run_model into the worker future, caught by the broad Exception handler at 2191. ALL evals fail with the same misleading error.
Step 3: User sees N failures, no clear root cause; root cause requires reading the evals.json file by hand to spot the typo.
Failure outcome: confusing failure mode for a config typo that should be caught at load time.
findings.recommendation: Add a load-time validator (jsonschema or hand-rolled): require id:int, name:str-without-slashes, files:list[str], assertions:list. Fail fast with a precise message.

findings.title: Section parsing brittleness: a model output with `**Rewrite**` inside an inline phrase or with a different cap (e.g. `**REWRITE**`) bypasses extract_generated_text and breaks every rewrite-related assertion silently
evidence: section_text matches `^\*\*{header}\*\*\s*$` MULTILINE|IGNORECASE. The IGNORECASE flag makes case insensitive — fine.

BUT: it requires the bold marker to be on its own line. A model that writes '**Rewrite:** ...' inline (with a colon, common in markdown), or '## Rewrite', or '### Rewrite', or '**Rewrite**\n\n' followed by no content — is not matched.
Trigger: a perfectly valid model rewrite that uses '## Rewrite' heading instead of '**Rewrite**' bold-line.
Step 1: extract_generated_text returns the WHOLE output (the audit + rewrite + chitchat) as `generated`.

Step 2: 'no-em-dashes-in-rewrite' check at line 1541 evaluates `'—' not in generated` — but `generated` is now the whole audit, which by design quotes the input's em-dashes. The check fails even if the rewrite itself has none.

Step 3: 'rewrite-passes-balanced-grader' at line 1551 calls GRADE.regrade(generated, depth='balanced') against the audit text — graders (no-manufactured-insight, no-signposted-conclusions) flag the audit's commentary, not the rewrite. The check fails not because the rewrite is bad but because the grader is grading the wrong text.
Step 4: pass_rate drops sharply for that eval; build_performance_report shows a regression. The PR's regression detection fires false positives on a benign formatting choice.
Failure outcome: pass-rate metric is sensitive to model formatting whim, undermining iteration-to-iteration comparability.
findings.recommendation: Allow heading variants (## / ### prefixes), require the rewrite section to be the LAST section, or require the model to use a fenced block. Document expectations in the prompt itself.

residual_risks: ThreadPoolExecutor with subprocess.run inside threads is the wrong concurrency primitive when subprocesses need to be cancelled; the codebase will keep accumulating cancellation/cleanup hazards until this is migrated to Popen + a signal-aware supervisor.

humanise/grade.py is imported once at module load via importlib (line 1240-1249); any test or dev script that mutates GRADE.* state will leak across iteration runs in the same process, since GRADE is a module-level singleton.

The PR introduces a 'comparative report' that becomes a public artefact (auto-published to README.md via update_readme_performance_block). Any data-quality bug in the harness now writes directly to a public surface — there is no review gate.

Subprocess call uses cwd=ROOT and the executor inherits the parent's filesystem permissions; a malicious skill prompt that bypasses claude's allowedTools could read repo secrets. claude --allowedTools Read,Bash is permissive — Bash with cwd=ROOT can do anything the user can do.
testing_gaps: No integration test exists for run_iteration end-to-end; failure modes adv-1 through adv-6 are reachable only by running the script for real with crafted inputs.
No test for what happens when subprocess.run raises TimeoutExpired (the most common failure on flaky models). The exception path is exercised only by a real timeout in production.
No test for KeyboardInterrupt during run_iteration; the cancellation path is unverified.
No test for malformed evals.json or corpus.json (duplicate ids, missing files, wrong types).
No test for re-entrancy: running iteration N+1 while the iteration N viewer is still alive.
No assertion that the determinism claim holds: there is no test that runs the same corpus twice and verifies group_summary is byte-identical.
No test for the case where every input file is empty (the no-input-draft-required assertion exists but no e2e check for it).
